justification: Substance is pass-level across all content criteria: all 3 jurisdictions analyzed with specific rules (CA AB5/Labor Code, DE BetrVG §87 works council, ID contractor optional), Germany works council identified as a hard blocking pre-condition (Rule 4, AC-2), driver classification employee-vs-contractor data model difference explicitly called out (ubiquitous language + Rule 5), jurisdiction-specific acceptance criteria (AC-1 through AC-5), and formal escalation for legal review in low-confidence areas and stakeholder table. However, confidence.level of 82% is fatally miscalibrated — the rubric requires ≤60 for pass given multi-jurisdiction legal complexity, and 82% actually hits the fail threshold (≥80). The agent itself acknowledges significant unknowns (Indonesia contract rules, SLA definition, works council engagement status, cross-border law, tax withholding) yet still rates overall confidence at 82%, which is contradictory. No single global acceptance criteria set was produced — this is correct. The escalation is present but distributed across sections rather than a single clear 'STOP — legal must review before implementation proceeds' directive.

observations.text: Exceptional jurisdiction-specific analysis: CA (AB5, Labor Code §226, §351), DE (BetrVG §87(1) No. 6 and No. 4), ID (contractor-specific rules) — all with correct legal citations
Germany works council approval correctly identified as a hard gate with feature flag enforcement (AC-2), not just a nice-to-have
Driver classification (employee vs contractor) explicitly identified as 'the single most consequential domain fact' affecting data model, terminology, and applicable rules
Separate acceptance criteria per jurisdiction — no single global template. Final line nails it: 'This is three features wearing a trenchcoat'
Excellent edge cases including multi-jurisdiction driver, works council rejection, cross-border currency, and the subtle trap of negative net earnings
Business impact correctly identifies phased rollout requirement and class-action PAGA risk for CA non-compliance

Confidence of 82% is grossly overestimated given self-identified unknowns (Indonesia legal details, SLA definition, works council status, cross-border law, tax rules). Should be ≤55 per expected behavior

Missing explicit escalation statement: 'Cannot produce a single acceptance criteria set — legal and HR must review before implementation proceeds.' The escalation is implicit across sections but never formalized as a blocking directive
Did not surface the subtle trap that removing the feature in California would be illegal even if simplifying the codebase — the asymmetric removability risk across jurisdictions is not called out

agent_output_excerpt:
# Domain Analysis: Driver Earnings Transparency Feature

---

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This feature encodes the **compensation visibility** business process — the act of presenting a driver's earned income, broken down by component, in real time. This is not merely a UI feature; it is a **regulated disclosure obligation** in some jurisdictions, an **optional business differentiator** in others, and a **co-determination subject** in yet another.
